summary: Jeff Atwood explores the controversial use of DoEvents in .NET WinForms development for creating responsive user interfaces. He presents a pragmatic technique of moving heavy operations from Form Load to the first Paint event with DoEvents to improve perceived performance. While acknowledging that DoEvents is considered poor practice by many developers who advocate for proper threading, Atwood argues it represents a reasonable tradeoff for simple scenarios. He contrasts the ease of DoEvents against the genuine difficulty of implementing correct multithreaded solutions, ultimately suggesting that even Microsoft recommends DoEvents for simple problems while waiting for better threading support in .NET 2.0.
key_points: Moving heavy operations from Form Load to the first Paint event with DoEvents dramatically improves perceived performance by giving users immediate visual feedback
DoEvents is a legacy VB technique that Microsoft still considers legitimate for simple UI yielding scenarios, despite criticism from threading purists
The danger of DoEvents is that it can cause unexpected re-entrancy issues when users interact with the UI during processing
Proper threading with Control.Invoke is the 'correct' solution but introduces significant complexity around synchronization and shared state
For complex scenarios, modeling UI and background threads as separate processes communicating only through messages avoids shared memory pitfalls
money_quotes: Threading is a manly approach for tough guys, and it will put hair on your chest – but you may not have any left on your head when you're done.
Never underestimate the power of perceived performance.
Threading is hard. Dangerous, even. It's not a coding burden you take on lightly.
You've never truly debugged an app until you've struggled with an obscure threading issue.
When things start happening in asynchronous, indeterminate order instead of the deterministic 1-2-3 order you'd expect... things get absurdly difficult really quickly.
Yielding on the UI thread is a legitimate Windows programming practice. It always has been.
The button responds to the event and starts another processing thread but it isn't actually a thread here.
key_insight: Perceived performance matters to users, and sometimes a 'dirty' hack like DoEvents is a reasonable tradeoff against the genuine complexity and danger of proper multithreaded programming.
